item.title: Friday Squid Blogging: Japanese Divers Video Giant Squid
item.author: Bruce Schneier
item.content:encoded: As usual, you can also use this squid post to talk about the security stories in the news that I haven’t covered.

item.title: Optimizing RIPEMD-160 with SIMD – Arm Neon and Beyond
item.author: Unknown
item.content:encoded: I have a hobby project – ecloop – a Bitcoin keys "calculator" designed to find Bitcoin Puzzles, verify brain wallets and so on. The mathematical chances of finding a private key from a used address tend to zero. I am interested in this program as a set of tricks over elliptic curve (secp256k1) and as a way to practice programming close to the processor (fast 256-bit arithmetic), so I continue to develop it from time to time.To calculate Bitcoin address from Private key, several operations need to be performed:calculate the point on the elliptic curve (Public key) – .calculate SHA256 from PubKey: (P.y % 2 == 0 ? 0x02 : 0x03) + P.xcalculate RMD160 from the resulting SHA256.The result will be the so-called , which is then encoded into a Bitcoin address using or .As slow as elliptic curve operations are, the slowest part of address generation is the RMD160 calculation. In fact, it takes about half the running time (SHA256 on modern processors has hardware acceleration).Almost all modern processors have SIMD support: AVX2 / AVX512 on amd64 and NEON / SVE2 on arm64. That's why I decided that it would be nice to speed up RMD160 by implementing it in the form of parallel calculations. Moreover, I have never written SIMD code before and I was interested to try it out.My main computer is a MacBook on Apple Silicon (M-chips). Initially I wanted to implement RMD160 SIMD on SVE (256 bit / 8 lane), but it turned out that Apple chips do not support SVE 🤦 (it should be clarified that M2 chip implements ARMv8.6 standard, while SVE was added in ARMv8.2, but as optional), so I had to use Neon (128 bit / 4 lane). If this is not the case, I'm wrong somewhere and there is a way to run SVE instructions on M-chips – I'd be happy for comments.RIPEMD-160 (RMD160) is a cryptographic hash function that creates a 160-bit hash from arbitrary data. It was developed as a secure alternative to earlier algorithms such as MD5 and SHA-1. RMD160 is widely used in blockchain technology, especially in Bitcoin, where it is used to create wallet addresses: the public key is first hashed using SHA-256 and then RMD160 to increase security and reduce length.The RMD160 algorithm consists of 5 rounds, each round includes basic logic functions, cyclic shifts (ROTL) and addition. A special feature of RMD160 is that each round is executed in two parallel branches: the main (left) branch and the parallel (right) branch. These two branches use different constants, word order and logic functions, and then their results are combined.There is a classicC-implementation of RMD160 using a bunch of macros to declare rounds, logical functions, etc., but such code is hard to read, so I prefer the Golang implementation, which I have previously ported to . I plan to do further porting to Neon on this codebase.Stepping back a bit, SIMD instructions don't look like something extremely complex, but they don't have "syntactic sugar", so instead of should be written something like . There are such special functions for every standard operation × count of numeric types (u/i 8/16/32/64 f16/32/64).RMD160 (as well as other hash functions) should not be too difficult to port to SIMD, because there are no branching in their algorithms. In essence, the algorithm remains the same, only all operations need to be changed to SIMD specific instructions.To understand how to write using Neon we should start with the simplest possible program, e.g. multiply 42 × 2. Since SIMD is a parallel computations, its operations are applied to the whole vector at once and the result should be the same in separate parts of the vector. To be sure of this, the result can be displayed in the console.In general, I think the idea of how SIMD calculations work is clear. Further will be used often to check the correctness of the hashing algorithm.The RMD160 calculation uses 5 basic functions and ROTL, everything else is a shuffling of data in a certain order. GPT has rewritten these macros and I have verified that they are correct:Golang implementation consists of 5 big loops that make 16 left and right rounds each, inside each operation input data is mixed at index and ROTL is performed at index . Reference C-implementation uses a bunch of consecutive macros, which makes it harder to read (in my opinion).In general, if we rewrite the code directly using Neon instructions, the compilation crashes with the error that and want to work with a known rotation value (the second argument) at the moment of compilation. See:So I have to use the macro version (C-reference), because there the indexes are passed directly (the last argument in the FF macro) and expanded into constant values at compile time. Maybe this change is for the better (we will see why later).If we look at the RMD160 rounds, the same actions happen there, but the following changes: base function, constant, data index and pivot. In general the round macro was above (I took a slightly different code from Github). My goal is to port the round macro to SIMD.In round, we add up 4 variables, do a ROTL + another addition, and a separate ROTL for another variable. Since the "+" operation is not in SIMD, we need to use special instructions.I added some macros for vector addition and described the round itself:In the macro, , , , , are the state variables, is the value after the base function computation, is the uint32 index data for the current iteration, is a constant and is the rotation value for ROTL. loads a constant into a vector (same value in all 4 lanes). Earlier we wrote code to multiply a vector by a number, for this purpose is used. Logically, the instruction to add a number to a vector should be , but it does not exist. Instead it should be written in vaddq_u32(vec1, vdupq_n_u32(2)) style (if anyone knows why this is like this – write in comments).Then, based on this round macro, we can define left and right rounds. The code here is similar to any other macro implementation (except that I made the round names as /).Now, using these macros, we can write the first iteration of the first round and compare it with the working implementation. If everything is OK, then we can copy the whole round, check it, and then all the remaining rounds. I compared the result with the function I made earlier.First left-round, first iteration:It should be noted that hash functions are usually tested on zeros data (for simplicity). Data in hash functions are processed by blocks. A block in RMD160 is 32×16 = 512 bits. can be called several times with the same state (it changes) and new data – for cases if you need to calculate the hash of a message larger than one round. In my task (address generation) all messages are placed in one block. The result of the first round is the current implementation:In general these values ± are similar to the values from the current version, differ by one offset. This is not a problem, since there are 5 variables and the offsets will be aligned by the end. Just a difference in the implementations.I won't write all the rounds, there are 80 of them on each side (160 total) – it would be too long. Left and right rounds are independent of each other and can be calculated in any order: either first all left / all right, or alternate left / right, or alternate iterations within a round. This will not affect the final result.At the end of the RMD160 block, we need to combine the old state with the local state – this is also three additions with index offsets.The final step is to change the endian of the values (RMD160 uses the different endian) and unload the values from the vector into the resulting array.That is all – can be used further where needed (, , , are counted hashes – 4 at once).To summarize this section, the complete parallel RMD160 algorithm looks like this:initialize a state of length 160 bit × 4 lanes ()split the message (data) into blocks of 512 bits × 4 lanes ()iterate rmd160 rounds until the data runs out ( reads the data into the vector itself)change endian in the final stateunload the vector of the final state into a hash array ()Now it remains to measure the reason for this code – to compare the performance of the original and SIMD code. For this purpose I made a small benchmark:I compiled both programs with and ran (on a basic Apple M2):The Neon version (128 bit / 4 lanes) is 55% faster. Which is a cool result, but it is sad that M-chips doesn't have SVE for 256/512 bit (8/16 lanes), it would be even better!While I was writing about the results above, I was to see what would happen if I change rounds order in RMD160. The original order of rounds was as follows: first all left rounds, then all right rounds. I thought this was good for the processor, because it seemed to require less "context switching".I changed the order of rounds to alternating left and right rounds (L1 / R1, L2 / R2) and performance increased significantly. I initially thought there was a data error, but (as tests) says everything is fine.I decided to try alternating iterations (L1_1 R1_1 L1_2 R1_2 R1_2 etc). To be honest, rearranging 160 lines is not the most fun, but the result surprised me even more.Comparison of different rounds / iterations placements (3 tests of RMD160 function itself and a full cycle of logic):It's a mystery to me why it works this way and maybe there is an even more effective arrangement? Who knows write in the comments.Initially I didn't have this in my plans, but RMD160 algorithm on macros was quite abstract and further porting to AVX2 looked quite simple. The main difference between AVX2 and Neon (except for a different instruction set) is the vector size – 256 bit vs 128 bit – so we can count 8 hashes in parallel (vs 4 in Neon).Now the following Neon instructions are used directly in the algorithm code: vector type (), state initialization via , endian-swap and load / dump data into the vector.I'm moving this things to macros (just in case I added prefix to avoid conflicts with other files):And update the current code to something like this:Of course it's already quite magical, but there are not really many changes. Now I should add redefined macros for AVX2. In addition, I wrapped a series of architecture-specific macros in . In fact, I have single code for RMD160 algorithm and the necessary macros are connected depending on which processor program is compiled on.The main differences are:different vector type (8 lanes instead of 4 in Neon) and names of intrinsicsAVX2 has no Bitwise NOT, so I had to add it separately as .no separate function for endian-swap, but there is a more generalized function to rearrange bits in a given order - (first argument - where to rearrange bits, second argument - how to rearrange). makes it more convenient to load data into different lanes, in Neon we had to use a temporary array (the variant with setting each lane separately () is slower).The rewriting of macros was almost handled by GPT, I just checked the correctness once again.I have a small Fanless PC with Linux on Intel N100 that I use for native application testing. On it I ran the benchmark written earlier and got the following results:8 lanes of AVX2 and correct arrangement of rounds in the algorithm (zebra iterations) gives 320% increase in the number of hashes per second compared to the original code. What is interesting is that AVX2 on Intel N100 runs 20% faster than Neon on Apple M2 (mainly because of vector size). Overall speedup: 5.45M it/s vs 7.73M it/s (+42%).In above, I left the section with so that the compilation would not work on unsupported systems. In general, this is not good and I would like the program to work everywhere (mainly for potential runs in VMs). Since the whole algorithm is already written in macros, it is not difficult to add a new implementation. I just redefine all macros to vector size 1 and as the "vector" type. In reality, the program will work with a single array, which from the point of view of memory in C is about the same as just .I ran it & checked it – it works correctly, except that the speed became a bit faster due to new order of rounds. It is not difficult to add implementation for AVX512 (16 lanes) in the same way, but I don't have a processor to test it, so I didn't do it, and without it the article was quite long.SIMD programming turned out to be easier than it seemed. The necessary intrinsics can be "googled" in GPT, it is difficult to search for them yourself because there are thousands of combinations. RMD160 itself is mostly used in cryptocurrencies (at least I don't know other popular usages), so the usefulness of the obtained code outside the learning factor is questionable.SIMD calculations give a good boost to execution speed, but of course you should take into account the specifics of the task: you have a lot of data to process and these data have same size. It makes no sense to use SIMD if some data to be hashed fits into 1 block and others into 100 (for example, when processing files). Also a main program should be able to process data in batches.Final code as single file on Github.

item.title: Optimizing RIPEMD-160 with SIMD – Arm Neon and Beyond
item.author: Unknown
item.content:encoded: I have a hobby project – ecloop – a Bitcoin key "calculator" designed to find Bitcoin puzzles, verify brain wallets, and so on. The mathematical chances of finding a private key from a used address tend toward zero. I'm interested in this program as a collection of tricks involving the elliptic curve (secp256k1) and as a way to practice low-level programming (fast 256-bit arithmetic), so I continue to develop it from time to time.To calculate a Bitcoin address from a private key, several operations need to be performed:Calculate the point on the elliptic curve (public key): .Compute SHA-256 from the compressed public key: (P.y % 2 == 0 ? 0x02 : 0x03) + P.x.Compute RIPEMD-160 from the resulting SHA-256 hash.The result is the so-called , which is then encoded into a Bitcoin address using either or .As slow as elliptic curve operations are, the slowest part of address generation is the RIPEMD-160 (RMD160) calculation. In fact, it takes about half of the total execution time (SHA-256 is hardware-accelerated on modern processors).Almost all modern processors have SIMD support: AVX2 on amd64 and Neon on arm64. That's why I decided it would be worthwhile to speed up RMD160 by implementing it using parallel calculations. Moreover, I had never written SIMD code before, so I was interested in trying it out.My main computer is a MacBook with Apple Silicon (M-series chips). Initially, I wanted to implement RMD160 SIMD using SVE (256-bit / 8-lane), but it turned out that Apple chips do not support SVE 🤦. (To clarify: the M2 chip implements the ARMv8.6 standard, while SVE was introduced in ARMv8.2 as an optional feature). So I had to use Neon (128-bit / 4-lane). If that's not the case and there is a way to run SVE instructions on M-series chips, I'd be happy to hear about it.RIPEMD-160 (RMD160) is a cryptographic hash function that produces a 160-bit hash from arbitrary data. It was developed as a secure alternative to earlier algorithms such as MD5 and SHA-1. RMD160 is widely used in blockchain technology, particularly in Bitcoin, where it is used to generate wallet addresses: the public key is first hashed using SHA-256, and then hashed again with RMD160 to improve security and reduce length.The RMD160 algorithm consists of five rounds. Each round includes basic logical functions, cyclic shifts (ROTL), and addition. A distinctive feature of RMD160 is that each round is executed in two parallel branches: the main (left) branch and the parallel (right) branch. These two branches use different constants, word orders, and logic functions, and their results are later combined.There is a classicC implementation of RMD160 that uses a bunch of macros to define rounds, logical functions, and so on—but such code is hard to read. So I prefer the Golang implementation, which I previously ported to . I plan to continue the porting to Neon based on this codebase.Stepping back a bit, SIMD instructions aren't extremely complex, but they lack "syntactic sugar" — so instead of writing , you need to write something like . There are special functions like this for every standard operation multiplied by the number of numeric types (u/i 8/16/32/64, f16/32/64).RMD160 (like other hash functions) should not be too difficult to port to SIMD because their algorithms contain no branching. Essentially, the logic stays the same; it's just a matter of replacing all operations with SIMD-specific instructions.To understand how to write using Neon, we should start with the simplest possible program, such as multiplying 42 × 2. Since SIMD involves parallel computations, its operations are applied to the entire vector at once, and the result should be the same across all parts of the vector. To verify this, the result can be printed to the console.In general, I think the concept of how SIMD calculations work is clear. From here on, will be used frequently to check the correctness of the hashing algorithm.The RMD160 calculation uses 5 basic functions and ROTL; everything else is a shuffling of data in a specific order. GPT has rewritten these macros, and I have verified that they are correct:The Golang implementation consists of 5 large loops, each performing 16 left and right rounds. Inside each operation, the input data is mixed at index , and ROTL is performed at index . The reference C implementation uses a series of consecutive macros, which makes it harder to read (in my opinion).In general, if we rewrite the code directly using Neon instructions, the compilation crashes with an error stating that and require a known rotation value (the second argument) at compile time. See:So, I have to use the macro version (C reference) because, in that case, the indexes are passed directly (the last argument in the FF macro) and expanded into constant values at compile time. Maybe this change is for the better (we will see why later).If we look at RMD160 rounds, the same actions are performed, but with the following changes: base function, constant, data index, and rotation. In general, the round macro was shown above (I took a slightly different version from GitHub). My goal is to port the round macro to SIMD.In the round, we add up 4 variables, perform a ROTL + another addition, and a separate ROTL for another variable. Since the "+" operation is not available in SIMD, we need to use special instructions.I added some macros for vector addition and described the round itself:In the macro, , , , , are the state variables, is the value after the base function computation, is the uint32 index data for the current iteration, is a constant, and is the rotation value for ROTL. loads a constant into a vector (the same value in all 4 lanes). Earlier, we wrote code to multiply a vector by a number, and for this purpose, is used. Logically, the instruction to add a number to a vector should be , but it does not exist. Instead, it should be written in vaddq_u32(vec1, vdupq_n_u32(2)) style (if anyone knows why this is the case – please leave a comment).Then, based on this round macro, we can define left and right rounds. The code here is similar to any other macro implementation (except that I named the rounds as /).Now, using these macros, we can write the first iteration of the first round and compare it with the working implementation. If everything is OK, we can copy the entire round, check it, and then move on to the remaining rounds. I compared the result with the function I created earlier.First left round, first iteration:It should be noted that hash functions are usually tested on zero data (for simplicity). Data in hash functions are processed in blocks. A block in RMD160 is 32×16 = 512 bits. can be called several times with the same state (which changes) and new data, for cases where you need to calculate the hash of a message larger than one round. In my task (address generation), all messages are placed in one block. The result of the first round compare to current implementation:In general, these values ± are similar to the values from the current version, differing by one offset. This is not a problem, since there are 5 variables, and the offsets will be aligned by the end. It's just a difference in the implementations.I won't write all the rounds, as there are 80 on each side (160 total) – it would result in an overly large code block. Left and right rounds are independent of each other and can be calculated in any order: either first all left / all right, or alternating left / right, or alternating iterations within a round. This will not affect the final result.At the end of RMD160 block, we need to combine the old state with the local state – this also involves three additions with index offsets.The final step is to change the endianness of the values (RMD160 uses a different endianness) and unload the values from the vector into the resulting array.That is all – can be used further where needed (, , , are the computed hashes — four at once).To summarize this section, the complete parallel RMD160 algorithm looks like this:Initialize a state of length 160 bits × 4 lanes ().Split the message (data) into blocks of 512 bits × 4 lanes ().Iterate RMD160 rounds until the data runs out ( reads the data into the vector itself).Change the endianness in the final state.Unload the vector of the final state into a hash array ().Now, it's time to measure the performance of this code – to compare the performance of the original and SIMD implementations. For this purpose, I created a small benchmark:I compiled both programs with and ran them (on a basic Apple M2):The Neon version (128-bit / 4 lanes) is 55% faster. This is a great result, but it's unfortunate that M-chips don't have SVE for 256/512 bits (8/16 lanes), as that would make it even better!While I was writing about the results above, I became to see what would happen if I changed the order of the rounds in RMD160. The original order of rounds was as follows: first all left rounds, then all right rounds. I thought this was good for the processor, because it seemed to require less "context switching".I changed the order of rounds to alternating left and right rounds (L1 / R1, L2 / R2), and the performance increased significantly. Initially, I thought there was a data error, but (as tests) confirms everything is fine.I decided to try alternating iterations (L1_1 R1_1 L1_2 R1_2 R1_2, etc.). To be honest, rearranging 160 lines is not the most fun, but the result surprised me even more.Comparison of different round/iteration placements (3 tests of RMD160 function itself and a full cycle of logic):It's a mystery to me why it works this way, and maybe there is an even more effective arrangement. Who knows? Please, write in the comments.Initially, I didn't plan to implement this, but RMD160 algorithm using macros was quite abstract, and further porting to AVX2 seemed fairly simple. The main difference between AVX2 and Neon (aside from the different instruction sets) is the vector size – 256 bits vs 128 bits – which allows us to process 8 hashes in parallel (vs 4 in Neon).Currently, the following Neon instructions are used directly in the algorithm code: vector type (), state initialization via , endian-swap, and load / dump data into the vector.I'm moving these things to macros (just in case, I added prefix to avoid conflicts with other files):And update the current code to something like this:Of course, it's already quite magical, but there aren't really many changes. Now, I should add redefined macros for AVX2. In addition, I wrapped a series of architecture-specific macros in . In fact, I have a single codebase for RMD160 algorithm, and the necessary macros are included depending on which processor the program is being compiled for.The main differences are:A different vector type (8 lanes instead of 4 in Neon) and different names for intrinsics.AVX2 has no Bitwise NOT, so I had to add it separately as .No separate function for endian-swap, but there is a more generalized function to rearrange bits in a given order: (the first argument specifies where to rearrange the bits, and the second specifies how to rearrange them). makes it more convenient to load data into different lanes, while in Neon we had to use a temporary array (the variant with setting each lane separately () is slower).The rewriting of the macros was mostly handled by GPT; I just checked the correctness once again.I have a small fanless PC running Linux on an Intel N100, which I use for native application testing. I ran the benchmark written earlier on it and got the following results:8 lanes of AVX2 and the correct arrangement of rounds in the algorithm (zebra iterations) result in a 320% increase in the number of hashes per second compared to the original code. What's interesting is that AVX2 on the Intel N100 runs 20% faster than Neon on the Apple M2 (mainly because of the vector size). Overall speedup: 5.45M it/s vs 7.73M it/s (+42%).In the above, I left the section with to prevent compilation on unsupported systems. In general, this is not ideal, and I would like the program to work everywhere (mainly for potential runs in VMs). Since the whole algorithm is already written in macros, adding a new implementation is not difficult. I simply redefine all macros to use a vector size of 1 and as the "vector" type. In reality, the program will work with a single array, which, from a memory perspective in C, is essentially the same as just using .I ran it and checked it – it works correctly, except that the speed became a bit faster due to the new order of rounds. It is not difficult to add an implementation for AVX512 (16 lanes) in the same way, but I don't have a processor to test it, so I didn't do it. Also, the article was already getting quite long, so I’m wrapping it up here.SIMD programming turned out to be easier than it seemed. The necessary intrinsics can be "Googled" in GPT; it is difficult to search for them yourself because there are thousands of combinations. RMD160 itself is mostly used in cryptocurrencies (at least I don't know of other popular use cases), so the usefulness of the obtained code outside of the learning factor is questionable.SIMD calculations give a good boost to execution speed, but, of course, you should take into account the specifics of the task: you need to have a lot of data to process, and these data should have the same size. It makes no sense to use SIMD if some data to be hashed fits into one block and others into 100 (for example, when processing files). Also, the main program should be able to process data in batches.The final code as a single file is available on Github.

item.title: Chinese AI Submersible
item.author: Bruce Schneier
item.content:encoded: A Chinese company has developed an AI-piloted submersible that can reach speeds “similar to a destroyer or a US Navy torpedo,” dive “up to 60 metres underwater,” and “remain static for more than a month, like the stealth capabilities of a nuclear submarine.” In case you’re worried about the military applications of this, you can relax because the company says that the submersible is “designated for civilian use” and can “launch research rockets.”“Research rockets.” Sure.

item.title: Function calling using LLMs
item.author: Martin Fowler
item.content:encoded: While LLMs excel at generating cogent text based on their training data, they may also need to interact with external systems. describes how we get them to construct external function calls to do this. The LLM does not execute these calls directly, instead it creates a data structure that describes the call, passing that to a separate program for execution and further processing. The LLM's prompt includes details about possible function calls and when they should be used.

item.title: Fake Student Fraud in Community Colleges
item.author: Bruce Schneier
item.content:encoded: Reporting on the rise of fake students enrolling in community college courses:The bots’ goal is to bilk state and federal financial aid money by enrolling in classes, and remaining enrolled in them, long enough for aid disbursements to go out. They often accomplish this by submitting AI-generated work. And because community colleges accept all applicants, they’ve been almost exclusively impacted by the fraud.The article talks about the rise of this type of fraud, the difficulty of detecting it, and how it upends quite a bit of the class structure and learning community.

item.title: Performance Profile Visualization Challenge
item.author: Alex Kladov
item.content:encoded: I love profiling! There’s a whole lot of different profiling tools available, and they provide a treasure trove of information. If you roughly know the slow area of the code, it’s usually not a problem to understand why it is slow. What I have trouble with though, is identifying the rough point of interest. Today I am pretty good at trisecting performance issues, and, if I make it a mission to identify the bottleneck, I usually manage to do it in a couple of hours. However, I don’t know a tool which can reliably identify bottlenecks automatically, in minutes. My problem is not that profilers don’t give enough information to me. My problem is that profilers give information, and its on the human to figure out what’s signal and what’s noise.Today, I’ve accidentally discovered a massive, 10x performance hole in TigerBeetle’s tests which can be traced to a single line of code. Which profiler can reliably identify the problematic line, and highlight it as single source of performance problems?Here’s a reproducible setup for the challenge:As you see, it takes quite some time to run. Now, I comment out a single line of code, and the runtime improves massively, almost 10x:Please find the line I’ve commented out! You of course can just check recent TigerBeetle pull requests, and it’s not wrong to do that, but I figured it’d be more fun if I don’t tell the answer right away :DDon’t worry if you don’t know Zig! The fact that the thing is written in an unfamiliar language, is built by a weird build system, and is run indirectly, is part of the challenge here! Ideally, the first line of profiling tooling shouldn’t require tweaking the system under test much.What unrealistic here is just how skewed this hotspot is — a single line of code is responsible for 90% of runtime! Usually, it is much less clear cut. But even in this ridiculous scenario, it took me an embarrassing amount of time to find it! Am I using the wrong tools incorrectly?Please post your solutions somewhere publicly, so that we all can learn to profile more efficiently!

item.title: Another Move in the Deepfake Creation/Detection Arms Race
item.author: Bruce Schneier
item.content:encoded: Recent research reveals that high-quality deepfakes unintentionally retain the heartbeat patterns from their source videos, undermining traditional detection methods that relied on detecting subtle skin color changes linked to heartbeats. The assumption that deepfakes lack physiological signals, such as heart rate, is no longer valid. This challenges many existing detection tools, which may need significant redesigns to keep up with the evolving technology. To effectively identify high-quality deepfakes, researchers suggest shifting focus from just detecting heart rate signals to analyzing how blood flow is distributed across different facial regions, providing a more accurate detection strategy.And the AI models will start mimicking that.

item.title: Zero to One: Learning Agentic Patterns
item.content:encoded: Learn common agentic design patterns and workflows for building robust, scalable AI applications, understanding when to use each.
